Name: Is there a distinct category of dual for verbal agreement with a dual subject?
Is non-agreement in number between the nominal subject and verbal predicate possible?
Can finite verbs agree in number with a nominal object?
Comment: The number of the object is indexed on the verb, e.g., 1SG meə-w° (singular object), 1SG meŋa-xəyu-n° (dual object), 1SG me-yə-n° (plural object); me- ‘to take, to do’.

Name: Can an adnominal property word agree with the noun in case?
Comment: However, number concord is more common.

Name: Is there non-agreement between an adnominal property word and the head noun?
Can adpositions be used to mark core verbal arguments?
Can non-finite clauses use a special means for core argument (S/A) marking?
Comment: In different-subject sentences, the lexical dependent subject is marked with the genitive.

Name: Can non-finite clauses use a special means for core argument (P) marking?
Comment: The object of transitive verbs is marked with the accusative both in finite as well as non-finite clauses.

Name: Do the characteristics of the object referent (topicality, animacy, definiteness) influence object marking?
Does object marking correlate with aspectuality?
Does object marking depend on the verb conjugation form?
Comment: Objects are typically marked with the accusative case. However, in the imperative also the nominative case is possible.

Name: Is there differential subject marking?
Can S and A be conflated morphologically across clause boundaries, i.e., act as a syntactic pivot?
Can S and P be conflated morphologically across clause boundaries, i.e., act as a syntactic pivot?
Is there grammatical marking of habituality?
Is there grammatical marking of frequentativity?
Comment: There are the frequentatie-iterative-multiplicative suffixes -or, -ur, -er indicating that a situation is repeated, habitual or dispersive.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of progressivity?
Is it common to use a verb in the present tense for future time reference?
Is there grammatical marking of the immediate future?
Comment: The future tense conveys both immediate as well as remote future.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of the pluperfect?
Comment: Whereas imperfective verbs typically have progressive reading, the perfective verbs usually indicate remote past. In addition, a perfective verb may be used to denote an event, which occurred before another event in the past.

Name: Can aspect be expressed overtly in the negative marker?
Comment: Aspectual morphology is expressed on the lexical verb.

Name: Can tense be expressed overtly in the negative marker?
Is there grammatical marking of mirativity?
Comment: The inferential evidential is also used in the mirative or exclamative function.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of a desire or wish?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to irrealis?
Are verbs marked with a non-indicative mood in counterfactual conditional clauses?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of deontic necessity?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of epistemic possibility?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of deontic possibility?
Comment: "In the 2nd person the meaning of the necessitative may be close to permissive (Nikolaeva 2014:91)".

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb used to direct commands/wishes, etc. to the 3Sg?
Is there morphological marking on the verb used to direct commands/wishes, etc. to the 1Sg?
Comment: The horative is used to express exhortations to the first person; it appears in 1SG, 1DU and 1PL.

Name: Is it common to use the imperative/jussive in complement clauses introduced by predicates expressing wish/desire/will?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of apprehension?
Is there a reflexive conjugation?
Comment: The reflexive conjugation is formed based on the reflexive stem.

Name: Can non-singular 2nd and 3rd person have the same person/number marking or morphosyntactic treatment?
Can the 3rd person plural verb form and impersonal (passive) verb form be encoded in the same way?
Are 2nd person singular verb forms commonly used with a generic meaning?
Can an infinitival verb form be used in a directive function?
Can verbs in purposive subordinate clauses be infinitive forms?
Comment: There is the purposive converb (final gerund).

Name: Are there sentences without overt verbal arguments?
Comment: The subject (god/sky/wheather) can be omitted although it is more common to express the subject.

Name: Is a copula commonly used for predicate nominals in the 3rd person singular forms of the present tense?
Is a copula commonly used for predicate nominals in the past tense?
Comment: The copula is not required in the past and present; the past is expressed on the adjective or noun, as in pidər° xanʹena-nə-sʹ° (you hunter-2SG-PST) 'you were a hunter' (Nikolaeva 2014:254).

Name: Can locative constructions commonly occur without a copula?
Is the same coordinating conjunction commonly used for NPs, VPs, and clauses?
Comment: Coordinating conjunctions can be found but this is not the commonest coordination strategy.

Name: Does existential negation have a special negator?
Comment: There are two existential copulas: tənʹa° (affirmative) and yəŋku (negative). Cf. standard negation: pida nʹī məncara-q (he NEG work-CONNEG) 'He did not work / he is not working'.

Name: Can standard negation be asymmetric in the present tense?
Can non-finite dependent clauses use the standard negation construction?
Comment: Modal, purposive, and conditional coverbs can be negated like finite verbs.

Name: Is there a special negator that combines with non-finite forms?
Comment: For instance, n'iwa negates action nominals.

Name: Are there special negative non-finite forms?
Is there an impersonal passive construction?
Comment: Only transitive verbs are passivized.

Name: Are there non-finite forms specializing in the relativization of particular core participants, e.g., subjects (S/A), direct objects (P), absolutive participants (S/P)?
Are there non-finite forms that can relativize all core participants (S/A/P) and possibly also some non-core participants?
Are there non-finite forms that can relativize at least some non-core participants but cannot relativize all core participants?
Is there a specialized negative participial form, which cannot be analyzed as a specific participial marker + negative marker?
Is there a future participle?
Are there participles that can encode their subject with possessive suffixes?
Comment: When agreeing with the head noun, participles can take possessive suffixes.

Name: Are there participles that must agree with the nouns they modify in case and number in all contexts?
Comment: See UT060.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and action nominalizations?
Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and agentive nominalizations?
Comment: There is no special agentive nominalization form, present participles are used regularly as agentive nominalizations.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and converbs?
Is OV the neutral order in affirmative imperative clauses?
Is it possible to place an object between the negative marker and the main verb in imperative constructions?
Is there diminutive (hypocoristic) marking on verbs?
Comment: The diminutive marking on verbs expresses the meaning 'for some time, for a while'.

Name: Can modifiers of action nominalizations in agent and patient roles both be encoded like possessors?
Can the modifier of action nominalizations in the patient role be encoded like a direct object?
Do core adjectives agree with the subject noun in number in the predicative position?
Is there a bound superlative degree marker on property words?
Comment: There is no synthetic superlative. Superlatives are formed with the help of adverbs expressing 'very', 'most', e.g. s'am'anəxət° 'most'.

Name: Can adpositions have person/number marking?
Comment: Pronominal object can be marked in the postposition.

Name: Are there adpositions that show a tripartite system?
Comment: Local postpositions usually appear in the locative (n´ana 'at'), dative (nʹah  'to'), (ablative nʹad° 'from') and prolative form (nʹamna 'along').

Name: Is there case compounding?
Are there morphological cases only used with pronouns?
Does the numeral modification of a noun have an effect on its case selection?
Is the singular form of the noun also used with numerals greater than 1?
Comment:
With numerals the quantified noun can stand in the singular or plural. In Tereshchenko (1965), it is usually in the singular. However, the speakers of the modern language prefer the plural, though may accept
the singular as well.

Name: Is there a dedicated case that expresses indirect object/recipient/goal (different from a local case)?
Comment: The dative case also expresses lative meanings.

Name: Is there a dedicated case that expresses a terminative meaning?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses the absence of a referent (abessive)?
Comment: There is a caritive adverbial form that ends in -s'iq.

Name: Is there a dedicated case that expresses motion along or by a referent (prolative)?
Is there a locative case that expresses goal?
Comment: The dative case (-n°h ~ -t°h) is used; it is regarded as a local case.

Name: Is there a locative case that expresses location?
Comment: There is the locative case, which additionally expresses instrumental and comitative meanings, means of transportation as well as cause.

Name: Is there a locative case that expresses source?
Is there case syncretism for goal and location marking?
Is there case syncretism for location and source marking?
Are there two sets of local cases?
Are there three or more sets of local cases?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses the means by which something is carried out (instrumental) and/or accompaniment (comitative)?
Comment: The locative case may be used in both functions.

Name: Is there a case that expresses the means by which something is carried out (instrumental), which is different from a case that expresses accompaniment (comitative) or other relations?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses the result of a change of state (translative) and/or a state of being (essive)?
Comment: The derivational suffix -ngæ appears in a similar function, e.g. li 'bone', lingæ 'as a bone, for a bone'.

Name: Is there a case that expresses a state of being (essive), which is different from a case that expresses the result of a change of state (translative) or other relations?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses equativity?
Comment: However, there is an adverbial form marked by the suffix -(də)r'em.

Name: Is there an inflectional nominal marker for expressing benefactive or destinative semantics independent from the dative?
Comment: The predistinative is used.

Name: Is there (in addition to the basic declension) a declension different from a possessive declension?
Is it possible to use the singular with the paired body parts/clothing/accessories that accompany them?
Comment: Paired objects typically stand in plural, still, dual may be encountered as well - 'my eyes’ can be expressed either as sǣw°-xəyu-nʹi (DU) or as sǣw°-nʹi (PL).

Name: Can the marking of the possessor in the attributive construction vary?
Comment: Whereas the lexical possessor takes the genitive form, the pronominal possessor appears in the nominative form.

Name: Can the possessor in the attributive construction be encoded with the nominative?
Comment: Pronominal possessor appears in the nominative form.

Name: Are singular and plural possessee distinguished in the possessive declension?
Can a 2Sg possessive suffix be used to mark definiteness?
Can a 3Sg possessive suffix be used to mark definiteness?
Comment: "[T]he 3rd person singular possessive affixes express identifiability based on deixis and situational context" (Nikolaeva 2014: 68). Their usage in this function is, however, optional.

Name: Is there a human vs. non-human distinction in independent interrogative pronouns?
Does 'who' commonly appear as a relative pronoun used for animates other than humans?
Does 'who' commonly appear as a relative pronoun used for inanimates?
Can a demonstrative stem be used as a pronoun with a 3Sg human referent?
Are there possessive suffixes, the position of which varies in relation to case/number suffixes?
Are case and number marked cumulatively across the paradigm?
Are there different words for mother’s brother and father’s brother?
Are there different words for younger and older relatives for relations other than siblings?
Is there an animacy distinction for both concepts: 'new/young' and 'old'?
Is there a distinction for both 'warm' and 'cold' depending on whether reference is to weather or the temperature of objects?
Is there contrastive length for consonants?
Is there contrastive length for vowels?
Comment: /iː/, /uː/, /æː/

Name: Is there a contrast between short and long unstressed vowels?
Comment: All three vowels are pronounced long or half-long and restricted to word-initial syllables.

Name: Is there a contrast in the length of geminate consonants?
Comment: Geminates are not generally typical of Tundra Nenets; if identical consonants become adjacent in the course of morphological derivation, degemination occurs, e.g., nʹum ‘name’ > nʹumʹi ‘my name (1SG)’ (<nʹum-mʹi).

Name: Are there more than two phonological quantities?
Are there extralong syllables?
Is the main foot structure trochaic?
Comment: Bisyllabic trochaic feet aligned to the left.

Name: Is there lexically distinctive word stress?
Is there morphologically conditioned stress?
Is there fixed primary stress?
Comment: Stress is predictable from syllable and segmental structure. It falls on an initial syllable, syllables preceding a syllable with schwa, and non-final syllables precided by am unstressed syllable.

Name: Is there stress in heavy syllables?
Is there non-initial primary stress?
Is there palatal vowel harmony?
Comment: It represents a phonetic phenomenon.

Name: Is there velar vowel harmony?
Comment: It represents a phonetic phenomenon.

Name: Is there labial vowel harmony?
Are there closing diphthongs?
Are there opening diphthongs?
Comment: However, the vowel ǣ is pronounced as a raising diphthong in the Eastern dialects (Nikolaeva 2014:17).

Name: Is there a qualitative contrast between subsets of short and long vowels?
Are there full vs. reduced vowels as contrastive subsets?
Comment: The over-short vowel ə is typically pronounced as a very short a. The reduced vowel ° is either pronounced as a very short ə, i.e., mid central vowel, or it adds length to the preceding consonant (e.g., in word-final sequences C°q or C°h) or vowel.

Name: Is there a high front rounded vowel /y/?
Is there a mid-high front rounded vowel /ø/?
Is there a high back unrounded vowel /ɯ ɨ/?
Is there a mid-high back unrounded vowel /ɤ ɘ/?
Comment: There is only allophonic ɤ.

Name: Is there a low back rounded vowel /ɒ/?
Is there a contrast between mid front vowels (/e/ or /ɛ/) and low front vowels (/æ/ or /a/)?
Comment: There is no opposition, since there is only long /æː/.

Name: Are there word-initial consonant clusters?
Comment: There are no consonantal clusters word-initially and word-finally; three-consonantal clusters are not allowed.

Name: Are there word-internal consonant clusters in the syllable onset?
Comment: There are no consonantal clusters word-initially and word-finally; three-consonantal clusters are not allowed.

Name: Are there consonant clusters in the syllable coda?
Comment: The maximal syllable structure is (C)V(C).

Name: Are there word-internal intervocalic clusters of three or more consonants?
Comment: There are no consonantal clusters word-initially and word-finally; three-consonantal clusters are not allowed.

Name: Are there word-final clusters of three or more consonants?
Comment: There are no consonantal clusters word-initially and word-finally; three-consonantal clusters are not allowed.

Name: Are there palatalized alveolar consonants?
Are there palatalized labial and velar consonants?
Comment: Most labial consonants have a palatalized (palato-labial) variant, in Forest Nenets there are palatalized velars as well.

Name: Is there a palatal stop?
Is there a palatal nasal /ɲ/?
Is there a palatal lateral /ʎ/?
Is there a velar nasal /ŋ/?
Is there an alveolar affricate /t͡s/?
Is there a non-alveolar affricate (e.g., /t͡ʃ/, /t͡ɕ/)?
Is there a phonological contrast between an unvoiced alveolar sibilant /s/ and an unvoiced postalveolar sibilant /ʃ/?
Are there voiced plosives (e.g., /b g d/)?
Comment: b bʹ d dʹ c cʹ and k only occur in restricted positions, e.g., they do not stand word-initially or in a post-consonantal position, except that postconsonantal b appears as a result of the change of w in some stems

Name: Is there a glottal stop /ʔ/?
Is there a phonological contrast between plain and labial velar consonants (e.g., /k/ vs. /kʷ/)?
Is there a constraint on the word-initial alveolar trill /r/?
Comment: Like secondary consonants, r and rʹ do not occur word-initially, except in recent loanwords.

Name: Is there a laryngeal fricative /h/?
Is there a voiced velar fricative /ɣ/?
Is there a voiceless velar fricative /x/?
Is there a voiced dental fricative /ð/?
Is there a labio-velar approximant /w/?
Is there a voiceless labiodental fricative /f/?
Is there radical qualitative consonant gradation?
Is there quantitative consonant gradation?
